title: Docker
summary: A to-the-point review of the Docker an SDET gets asked about: images vs containers, the Dockerfile & layer caching, the container lifecycle, volumes & networking, Docker Compose, and — the part that wins the interview — using containers for reproducible, ephemeral test environments and CI. Short explanations, short examples.

sections.title: What Docker Is — Containers vs VMs
sections.bodyHtml:
A container packages an app with its dependencies so it runs the same everywhere. It shares the host OS kernel and isolates only the process, filesystem, and network — so it’s far lighter than a VM.

ContainerVirtual Machine
Isolatesprocess (shares host kernel)full OS (own kernel)
Size / startMBs, secondsGBs, minutes
Overheadnear-nativehypervisor

“Works on my machine” disappears: the image is the environment, so dev, CI, and prod run byte-identical dependencies.

For an SDET: the pitch is reproducibility — spin up the exact same browser/driver/DB stack locally and in CI, throw it away after, and never debug environment drift again.

sections.title: Images vs Containers & Layers
sections.bodyHtml:
• Image — a read-only template (the blueprint). Built from a Dockerfile, tagged like myapp:1.2.
  • Container — a running (or stopped) instance of an image, with a thin writable layer on top.
  • Registry — where images live (Docker Hub, ECR, GHCR); push/pull moves them.

An image is a stack of layers, one per build instruction. Layers are cached and shared between images — so ordering your Dockerfile well is the main build-speed lever.

Image vs container, in one line: the image is the class, the container is the object — one image, many containers.

sections.title: The Dockerfile & Build Caching
sections.bodyHtml:
A Dockerfile is the recipe for an image. Each instruction adds a layer.

FROM node:20-alpine            # base image
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./          # copy deps manifest FIRST
RUN npm ci                     # cached unless package*.json changes
COPY . .                       # then the source
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]      # default process
  • Cache order matters: copy the dependency manifest and install before copying source, so a code change doesn’t bust the (slow) dependency layer.
  • CMD vs ENTRYPOINT — CMD sets the default command (easily overridden); ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable, with CMD as its default args.
  • Multi-stage builds — build in a heavy image, copy only the artifact into a tiny runtime image; smaller, safer final images.
  • Use a .dockerignore to keep node_modules/.git out of the build context.
Common trap: putting COPY . . before RUN npm ci means every source edit reinstalls all dependencies — the #1 slow-build mistake.

sections.title: Running Containers
sections.bodyHtml:
The everyday commands an SDET should be fluent in:

CommandDoes
docker runcreate + start a container from an image
docker ps / ps -alist running / all containers
docker exec -itrun a command (e.g. a shell) inside a running container
docker logsview a container’s stdout/stderr
docker stop / rmstop / delete a container
docker build -tbuild an image from a Dockerfile
docker run -d --name web -p 8080:3000 -e NODE_ENV=test myapp:1.2
#        |    |            |             |
#   detached  name    host:container  env var

docker exec -it web sh        # shell into it
docker logs -f web            # follow the logs
docker rm -f web             # stop + remove
Ports: -p 8080:3000 maps host 8080 → container 3000. Without publishing a port the service isn’t reachable from the host.

sections.title: Volumes & Data Persistence
sections.bodyHtml:
A container’s writable layer is ephemeral — delete the container and its data is gone. Volumes persist data outside the container lifecycle.

  • Named volume — Docker-managed storage (-v mydata:/var/lib/postgresql/data); the right choice for databases.
  • Bind mount — maps a host directory into the container (-v $(pwd):/app); great for live-reloading source in dev.
  • tmpfs — in-memory only; fast and wiped on stop.
For tests: bind-mount your test code in for fast iteration, but keep test data ephemeral so every run starts from a clean, known state.

sections.title: Networking
sections.bodyHtml:
• bridge (default) — containers on the same user-defined bridge network reach each other by name (built-in DNS).
  • host — share the host’s network stack (no port mapping, less isolation).
  • none — no networking.

Inside a Compose network a service connects to another by its service name as the hostname — e.g. the app reaches the DB at postgres:5432, not localhost.

Classic gotcha: a container’s localhost is the container itself, not the host or another container — use the service/container name (or, from inside a container to the host, host.docker.internal).

sections.title: Docker Compose
sections.bodyHtml:
Compose defines a multi-container app in one YAML file and brings it all up with a single command — perfect for a test stack (app + DB + mock server).

services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["8080:3000"]
    environment:
      DB_URL: postgres://db:5432/test
    depends_on: [db]
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: test
    tmpfs: ["/var/lib/postgresql/data"]   # ephemeral test DB
  • docker compose up -d / down — start / tear down the whole stack.
  • depends_on controls start order (not readiness — add a healthcheck for that).
  • One network is created automatically, so services resolve each other by name.

sections.title: Docker for Testing & CI
sections.bodyHtml:
This is where containers earn their keep for an SDET — disposable, identical environments on every run.

  • Ephemeral dependencies — spin up a real Postgres/Redis/Kafka in a container for integration tests instead of mocking, then throw it away.
  • Testcontainers — a library (Java/.NET/Python/Node) that starts containers from your test code and tears them down after, with dynamic ports — no leftover state.
  • Selenium/Playwright in Docker — official browser images and Selenium Grid containers give consistent, headless browsers in CI.
  • CI — GitHub Actions / GitLab run jobs inside containers (services: / container:), so the build environment is version-pinned and reproducible.
// Testcontainers (Java) — a throwaway Postgres for one test class
@Container
static PostgreSQLContainer<?> db =
    new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16");
// db.getJdbcUrl() gives a dynamic URL; container is removed after tests
Say this: Testcontainers gives you real dependencies with the isolation of unit tests — no shared staging DB, no flaky cross-test contamination.

quiz.question: Container vs VM?
choices: A container shares the host kernel and isolates only the process — MBs, seconds; a VM virtualizes a full OS with its own kernel — GBs, minutes
A container virtualizes a full OS with its own kernel; a VM shares the host kernel and is lighter
Containers and VMs both run their own kernel, but containers boot faster due to caching
A VM isolates only the process while a container needs a hypervisor to run
quiz.correctAnswer: A container shares the host kernel and isolates only the process — MBs, seconds; a VM virtualizes a full OS with its own kernel — GBs, minutes
quiz.explanation: A container shares the host kernel and isolates just the process — MBs and seconds; a VM virtualizes a full OS with its own kernel — GBs and minutes.

quiz.question: Image vs container?
choices: Both are read-only templates; the container just has a newer tag than the image
An image is a running process; a container is the saved snapshot you push to a registry
The image is a read-only template (the class); a container is a running instance of it (the object) with a writable layer
An image is a container that has been stopped; a container is an image that is running
quiz.correctAnswer: The image is a read-only template (the class); a container is a running instance of it (the object) with a writable layer
quiz.explanation: The image is a read-only template (the class); a container is a running instance of it (the object) with a writable layer on top.

quiz.question: Why order Dockerfile instructions carefully?
choices: Docker runs instructions in parallel, so ordering decides which layer wins the race
Each instruction is a cached layer; copy and install dependencies before source so code changes don't bust the dependency layer
Instructions execute bottom-to-top, so the CMD must come before FROM to build
Ordering only affects the final image size, never the build speed or caching
quiz.correctAnswer: Each instruction is a cached layer; copy and install dependencies before source so code changes don't bust the dependency layer
quiz.explanation: Each instruction is a cached layer; copy the dependency manifest and install before copying source so code changes don’t bust the dependency layer.

quiz.question: CMD vs ENTRYPOINT?
choices: ENTRYPOINT sets the default command that is easily overridden; CMD is the fixed executable
CMD and ENTRYPOINT are aliases; both set the container's hostname at runtime
CMD sets the default command (easily overridden); ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable, with CMD supplying default arguments
CMD runs at build time while ENTRYPOINT runs only when you exec into the container
quiz.correctAnswer: CMD sets the default command (easily overridden); ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable, with CMD supplying default arguments
quiz.explanation: CMD sets the default command (easily overridden at run); ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable, with CMD supplying its default arguments.

quiz.question: How do you persist data?
choices: Use a volume (named volume for DBs, bind mount for live source); the writable layer is deleted with the container
Commit the container to a new image after every write to keep the data
Store data in the container's writable layer, which Docker persists automatically on stop
Use EXPOSE to map the data directory to the host so it survives removal
quiz.correctAnswer: Use a volume (named volume for DBs, bind mount for live source); the writable layer is deleted with the container
quiz.explanation: Use a volume (named volume for DBs, bind mount for live source) — the container’s own writable layer is deleted with the container.

quiz.question: How do two containers talk?
choices: Each container's localhost points to the others, so they connect via localhost and the port
They must publish ports to the host with -p and connect through the host's IP
On a shared user-defined/Compose network they resolve each other by service or container name via built-in DNS
They communicate only through a shared bind-mounted volume on disk
quiz.correctAnswer: On a shared user-defined/Compose network they resolve each other by service or container name via built-in DNS
quiz.explanation: On a shared user-defined/Compose network they resolve each other by service or container name via Docker’s built-in DNS — not localhost.

quiz.question: What does -p 8080:3000 do?
choices: Maps container port 8080 to host port 3000 in the reverse direction
Forwards host port 8080 to another container's port 3000 on the same network
Opens both ports 8080 and 3000 on the host for redundancy
Publishes (maps) host port 8080 to container port 3000 so the service is reachable from the host
quiz.correctAnswer: Publishes (maps) host port 8080 to container port 3000 so the service is reachable from the host
quiz.explanation: Publishes (maps) host port 8080 to container port 3000 so the service is reachable from the host.

quiz.question: What is a multi-stage build?
choices: Build in a heavy image, then COPY only the artifact into a small runtime image — smaller, more secure final images
Building one image that runs across multiple host stages of a CI pipeline
Splitting a Dockerfile into multiple files that are merged at runtime
Running several build stages in parallel to combine their layers into one large image
quiz.correctAnswer: Build in a heavy image, then COPY only the artifact into a small runtime image — smaller, more secure final images
quiz.explanation: Build in a heavy image, then COPY only the artifact into a small runtime image — smaller, more secure final images.

quiz.question: What is Docker Compose for?
choices: Defining and running a multi-container app from one YAML file with a single up/down
Compressing multiple images into a single layer to speed up registry pushes
Orchestrating containers across a cluster of hosts with automatic failover
Building a single image from several Dockerfiles in one command
quiz.correctAnswer: Defining and running a multi-container app from one YAML file with a single up/down
quiz.explanation: Defining and running a multi-container app (app + DB + mocks) from one YAML file with a single up/down.

quiz.question: How does Docker help testing?
choices: By caching test results inside image layers so reruns skip already-passing tests
It mocks all external dependencies automatically so no real services are needed
Reproducible, disposable environments — real dependencies via Testcontainers and consistent browsers in CI, with clean state every run
It runs tests faster by sharing one long-lived staging database across all test runs
quiz.correctAnswer: Reproducible, disposable environments — real dependencies via Testcontainers and consistent browsers in CI, with clean state every run
quiz.explanation: Reproducible, disposable environments — real dependencies via Testcontainers and consistent browsers in CI, with clean state every run.

quiz.question: Image too big — what do you do?
choices: Run more layers so Docker can compress them together into a smaller image
Use a slim/alpine base, multi-stage builds, a .dockerignore, and combine RUN steps to cut layers
Switch from images to bind mounts so nothing is stored in the image
Add a full OS base image so the layers deduplicate against the host
quiz.correctAnswer: Use a slim/alpine base, multi-stage builds, a .dockerignore, and combine RUN steps to cut layers
quiz.explanation: Use a slim/alpine base, multi-stage builds, a .dockerignore, and combine RUN steps to cut layers.

quiz.question: depends_on guarantees readiness?
choices: Yes — it waits for the dependency's healthcheck to pass before starting the service
Yes — it blocks until the dependent container's main process is fully initialized
No — it only controls start order; add a healthcheck (or app retry) to wait until the dependency is actually ready
No — it does nothing at all and is ignored unless you also set restart: always
quiz.correctAnswer: No — it only controls start order; add a healthcheck (or app retry) to wait until the dependency is actually ready
quiz.explanation: No — it only controls start order; add a healthcheck (or retry in the app) to wait until the dependency is actually ready.
